output_text:
Revised the section to tighten prose, fix tense inconsistencies, remove hedging, and sharpen the technical claims.

---

This section presents an adaptive model predictive control (MPC) framework for nonlinear systems. The controller continuously compares its one-step-ahead predictions against the observed plant output and uses the resulting prediction error to retune the internal model parameters online, maintaining closed-loop tracking performance under slow drift in the plant dynamics.

The self-evaluation module operates on a sliding window of the 50 most recent samples and computes a weighted prediction residual. The controller uses this residual to decide whether to trigger a parameter update; the triggering scheme suppresses unnecessary updates while still reacting quickly to substantial changes in the plant. The weighting kernel is a truncated exponential, with bandwidth selected from prior characterization of similar plants. The update law itself differs from our earlier work and is detailed in Section [X].

We evaluate the controller on a benchmark of 12 nonlinear plants whose parameters drift according to a slow random walk. The proposed scheme reduces integrated tracking error by 23% relative to a fixed-parameter MPC baseline, and its computational overhead stays within real-time bounds at the sampling rates considered.

The controller is also robust to measurement noise, outperforming the alternative adaptive schemes evaluated in our design study. We note one limitation: closed-loop stability under the proposed update law is assumed rather than formally proven, and we defer a Lyapunov-based analysis to future work.

---

Key changes:
- **Tense:** Unified to present tense (standard for describing a contribution); removed mixed past/future ("computed," "will reduce").
- **Run-on sentences:** Broke the four sprawling paragraphs into shorter, scannable claims.
- **Hedging removed:** "appears to be relatively robust" → "is robust"; "we believe this represents an advantage" → direct comparative claim; "approximately 23%" → "23%".
- **Filler cut:** "that we develop," "that we then use," "that we had implemented as a reference," "during the design phase," etc.
- **Limitation reframed:** Moved from a defensive "we acknowledge" to a forward-looking "we defer ... to future work," which reads as a scoping decision rather than a weakness.
- **Numerals:** "fifty" → "50" and "12" kept (consistent with technical-writing norm of digits for measurements/counts ≥10).

Uncertainty: I assumed (a) the 23% figure is exact rather than rounded — if it is actually approximate, restore "approximately"; (b) the comparative claim against alternative adaptive schemes is backed by reported numbers in the paper — if it was only an informal impression, soften to "compared favorably with" or remove. The "Section [X]" cross-reference is a placeholder you should fill in.
